sn11.4:1.0.1: At Sāvatthī. The Blessed One said this:sn.i.221
sn11.4:1.0.2: v.872"‘When face to face with VepacittiIs it, Maghava, from fear or weaknessThat you endure him so patiently,Listening to his harsh words?’
sn11.4:1.0.3: v.873"‘It is neither through fear nor weaknessThat I am patient with Vepacitti.How can a wise person like meEngage in combat with a fool?’
sn11.4:1.0.4: v.874"‘Fools would vent their anger even moreIf no one would keep them in check.Hence with drastic punishmentThe wise man should restrain the fool.’
sn11.4:1.0.5: v.875"‘I myself think this aloneIs the way to check the fool:When one knows one's foe is angryOne mindfully maintains one's peace.’
sn11.4:1.0.6: v.876"‘I see this fault, O Vāsavā,In practising patient endurance:When the fool thinks of you thus,"He endures me out of fear,"The dolt will chase you even moreAs a bull does one who flees.’sn.i.222
sn11.4:1.0.7: v.877"‘Let it be whether or not he thinks,"He endures me out of fear,"Of goals that culminate in one's own goodNone is found better than patience.
sn11.4:1.0.8: v.878"‘When a person endowed with strengthPatiently endures a weakling,They call that the supreme patience;The weakling must be patient always.
sn11.4:1.0.9: v.879"‘They call that strength no strength at all—The strength that is the strength of folly—But no one can reproach a personWho is strong because guarded by Dhamma.
sn11.4:1.0.10: v.880"‘One who repays an angry man with angerThereby makes things worse for himself.Not repaying an angry man with anger,One wins a battle hard to win.
sn11.4:1.0.11: v.881"‘He practises for the welfare of both,His own and the other's,When, knowing that his foe is angry,He mindfully maintains his peace.
sn11.4:1.0.12: v.882"‘When he achieves the cure of both—His own and the other's—The people who consider him a foolAre unskilled in the Dhamma.’
sn11.4:1.0.13: "So, bhikkhus, if Sakka, lord of the devas, subsisting on the fruit of his own merit, exercising supreme sovereignty and rulership over the Tāvatiṁsa devas, will be one who speaks in praise of patience and gentleness, then how much more would it be fitting here for you, who have gone forth in such a well-expounded Dhamma and Discipline, to be patient and gentle."
